title: DevOps Operations Research Note 2026-05-25: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations
headingPath: DevOps Operations Research Note 2026-05-25: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations
text: DevOps Operations Research Note 2026 05 25: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations Summary This page should define DevOps recovery as a chain of state layers, not as a single backup product. In a Kubernetes environment, control plane recovery starts with etcd because Kubernetes identifies etcd as the backing store for all cluster data and explicitly says etcd backed clusters need a backup plan [1][2]. The page should separate etcd snapshots, Kubernetes resources, persistent volume data, GitOps controller data, Terraform state, and application native data so the reader does not assume one tool recovers every layer. 1 2 The operating standard should be restore proof, not backup optimism. RTO and RPO targets need to be set from business needs, then mapped to backup frequency, retention, encryption, repository isolation, restore order, and test cadence [17][18][19]. Use this framing to explain why a green scheduled backup is only a signal that a job ran; it is not evidence that the team can rebuild a service inside its recovery objective. 1 2 Assumption for the planned page: Kubernetes clusters use etcd, workloads are mostly declarative, Velero v1.18 is the cluster resource/PV backup tool, Argo CD is a representative GitOps control plane, and Terraform state is a separate infrastructure recovery asset. 1 2 Keep the tone practical: name the layer being protected, the restore mechanism, the owner, the expected RPO/RTO, and the last successful restore exercise. 1 2 Do not claim cluster backup completeness unless the plan covers control plane state, persistent data, secrets/encryption keys, Git/IaC state, and application consistency. 1 2 Decision Matrix The decision matrix should compare mechanisms by recovery target, consistency, portability, operational risk, and expected operator action. etcd snapshots are the right control plane primitive when the cluster state itself must be recovered, but they do not by themselves prove application data consistency or cross cluster portability [2][3]. Velero resource backups and restores are better for namespace, resource, migration, and PV oriented recovery because Velero creates Restore objects, fetches backup metadata and volume data from its BackupStorageLocation, and applies a documented resource order [8][9]. 2 3 For persistent volumes, the matrix should force a choice between CSI snapshots, Velero File System Backup, and application native backups. CSI snapshots fit drivers with v1 VolumeSnapshot support, but portability can depend on matching CSI driver names and on snapshot durability outside the original storage system [7][10]. File System Backup is broader but less consistent because it reads live mounted filesystems and can require privileged/root node agent access, so databases and queues usually need either application hooks or native backup tooling in addition to platform backup [11][12]. 2 3 Use etcd snapshot when the question is: can the Kubernetes API/control plane state be rebuilt after control plane loss? Verify the snapshot with etcdutl snapshot status before trusting it [3][4]. 2 3 Use Velero resource restore when the question is: can namespaces, CRDs, RBAC, workloads, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, PVCs, and selected PV data be restored into the same or a replacement cluster [8][9]? 2 3 Use CSI snapshot when the storage driver supports Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot APIs and the runbook records the VolumeSnapshotClass, deletionPolicy, and cross cluster driver name requirement [7][10]. 2 3 Use File System Backup when the storage type lacks native snapshots or portability matters more than point in time consistency, and add application quiescing for write heavy systems [11][12]. 2 3 Use Terraform state backups before backend migration or major infrastructure changes, because Terraform state maps real infrastructure objects to configuration and backend changes can require state migration [14][15]. 2 3 Reference Architecture The reference architecture should use separate protection paths for separate state domains. Store encrypted etcd snapshots in a location that is outside the failed control plane nodes; store Velero backups and volume artifacts in a BackupStorageLocation with credentials that are scoped separately from application and cluster admin credentials; keep Git repositories and Terraform remote state protected as independent recovery assets; and document application native backups for consistency sensitive data stores [2][8][13][14][15][16]. This separation matters because a compromise, deletion, or misconfiguration in one layer should not erase every recovery option at once. 2 3 The storage portion of the architecture should be explicit instead of generic. StorageClass can encode reclaimPolicy, allowVolumeExpansion, mount options, binding mode, and provider specific parameters, while VolumeSnapshot support exists through CRDs and CSI drivers rather than the core API [6][7]. The page should include a table for each production StorageClass and VolumeSnapshotClass: provisioner, reclaimPolicy, snapshot class, deletionPolicy, supported volume modes, cross cluster restore notes, encryption status, and whether Velero uses CSI snapshots, data movement, or File System Backup. 2 3 Supported etcd snapshot template: ETCDCTL API=3 etcdctl endpoints "$ENDPOINT" snapshot save snapshot.db followed by etcdutl write out=table snapshot status snapshot.db [2][3][4]. 2 3 Supported Velero schedule template: velero schedule create <name schedule="CRON TZ=America/New York 0 3 " include namespaces <namespace list [8]. 2 3 Supported ad hoc backup from schedule template: velero backup create <backup name from schedule <schedule name [8]. 2 3 Document whether object store immutability is used with Velero; Velero v1.18 warns that backup metadata updates can conflict with some immutability configurations [8]. 2 3 Back up Terraform state before backend migration and avoid committing state to Git because state can contain sensitive information and needs locking plus secure access control [14][15]. 2 3 Restore Runbook The restore runbook should begin with incident classification, change freeze, scope selection, and restore point selection. If the control plane is intact and the loss is scoped to a namespace, CRD, application, or PVC, prefer a Velero restore into the existing or replacement cluster and inspect restore logs before reopening traffic [9]. If etcd quorum or the entire control plane is lost, restore etcd from a verified snapshot, treat the restored members as a new logical cluster, and reconfigure Kubernetes API servers if the etcd access URLs change [2][3]. 2 3 For etcd, the page should warn that snapshot restore can move revision history backward from the perspective of Kubernetes controllers and informers. The etcd recovery guide recommends revision bumps and marking revisions compacted in Kubernetes contexts to invalidate stale watch caches; this should be presented as an expert reviewed control plane operation, not as a routine namespace restore [3]. For Velero, the runbook should use the documented velero restore create workflow, note default restore order, and call out existing resource policy, namespace mappings, NodePort preservation, and PV/PVC restore mode before execution [9]. 2 3 Control plane restore template: etcdutl snapshot restore snapshot.db data dir <output dir bump revision <safe revision delta mark compacted ; choose the revision delta with the etcd/Kubernetes owner and document the rationale [3]. 2 3 Multi member etcd restore must restore every member from the same snapshot and set updated membership details such as name, data directory, initial cluster, token, and peer URLs [3]. 2 3 Application/resource restore template: velero restore create <restore name from backup <backup name wait ; then run velero restore describe <restore name and velero restore logs <restore name to review outcome [9]. 2 3 Namespace remap template: velero restore create <restore name from backup <backup name namespace mappings old ns:new ns when restoring into an isolated namespace for validation [9]. 2 3 Do not resume GitOps automation until restored objects, app health checks, storage attachments, secrets, ingress/LB behavior, and Terraform state expectations are reconciled. 2 3 Failure Scenarios The failure scenario section should show that the correct response depends on what was lost. Complete control plane loss is an etcd and API server recovery problem; namespace deletion is usually a Velero resource restore problem; PVC deletion is governed by PV reclaimPolicy and snapshot/FSB availability; a failed storage driver migration is a StorageClass and CSI portability problem; and a lost Terraform backend is an infrastructure state recovery problem [2][3][5][6][7][9][14][15]. The page should avoid vague labels such as "restore the cluster" and instead name the failed layer and the recovery authority for that layer. 2 3 Ransomware and credential compromise deserve a separate scenario because the attacker may target backup systems. The runbook should require out of band repository access review, backup integrity checks, and restoration into a clean target before reconnecting to production identities [20]. This is also where Velero immutability caveats matter: storage level immutability can protect data, but Velero may need to update metadata during backup finalization, so provider behavior and Velero compatibility need a lab tested design rather than an assumption [8]. 2 3 Scenario: all control plane nodes lost. Use verified etcd snapshot, restore all members from the same snapshot, rebuild API servers, update etcd servers or the load balancer if endpoints changed, then validate controller convergence [2][3]. 2 3 Scenario: accidental namespace deletion. Create an isolated restore if possible, review Velero logs, validate Secrets and ConfigMaps, then re enable ingress or GitOps sync [9]. 2 3 Scenario: PVC deleted. Check PV reclaimPolicy first; Retain may preserve the external asset for manual reclamation, while Delete can remove supported backend storage [5]. 2 3 Scenario: CSI snapshot restore into another cluster. Confirm VolumeSnapshot CRDs, snapshot controller, CSI driver capability, VolumeSnapshotClass, and matching CSI driver names on the destination cluster [7][10]. 2 3 Scenario: Terraform state loss or backend migration. Restore state from secure remote backend backup or the manual backup taken before migration; do not reconstruct state casually from memory because state binds Terraform resource instances to real objects [14][15]. 2 3 Operational Checklist The checklist should be written as a recurring operational control. Before an incident, record every backup schedule, backup owner, storage location, credential path, encryption mechanism, retention period, and restore test date. For Kubernetes, inventory StorageClasses, VolumeSnapshotClasses, Velero schedules, backup storage locations, namespaces included/excluded, and FSB annotations or default behavior [6][7][8][10][11][12]. For platform infrastructure, record Terraform backend type, lock behavior, state access controls, and the process for manually backing up state before migration [14][15]. 2 6 During restore exercises, measure real RTO and RPO rather than estimating. Pick a representative workload, restore it into an isolated namespace or disposable cluster, compare actual data age to the RPO target, time the restore path against the RTO target, review logs, and file defects for missing resources, broken CRDs, failed PV binds, slow data movement, or app inconsistency [9][17][18]. The page should encourage teams to fix these gaps while calm; an incident is the worst time to discover that a CRD was excluded or that a StorageClass in the target cluster does not match the backup. 2 6 Inventory commands: kubectl get storageclass , kubectl get volumesnapshotclass , kubectl n velero get schedule,backup,restore , and velero backup describe <backup name details . 2 6 Before major changes: take an etcd snapshot when control plane state is in scope; run an ad hoc Velero backup from the relevant schedule; and manually back up Terraform state before backend migration [2][8][15]. 2 6 During each restore exercise: record selected backup, target cluster, namespace mapping, StorageClass mapping, restore duration, data age, failed resources, manual interventions, and whether the result met RTO/RPO [9][17][18]. 2 6 Security checks: verify encrypted etcd snapshots, Kubernetes API data encryption posture, backup repository access controls, and offline or isolated backup copies [2][16][20]. 2 6 Close the loop: update runbooks, diagram changes, source quality metadata, and schedule definitions after every tool upgrade or storage driver change. 2 6 Common Pitfalls The most damaging pitfall is assuming that one backup layer covers another. An etcd snapshot protects Kubernetes API state, not necessarily application transaction consistency or portable PV data; a Velero namespace backup protects selected resources and volume artifacts, not the Terraform state that created the underlying infrastructure; and GitOps desired state does not replace live secrets, controller state, or historical data unless those assets are deliberately protected [2][8][9][13][14]. The page should repeat this because many outages begin with a false sense of coverage. 2 5 The second major pitfall is treating storage defaults as harmless. Dynamically provisioned PVs inherit the StorageClass reclaim policy and default to Delete if not specified, VolumeSnapshots depend on CSI support and deletion policies, Velero FSB reads live files and is less consistent than snapshot approaches, and Velero scheduled backups with owner references can behave badly when the schedule is removed [5][7][8][11]. These are not edge cases; they are exactly the defaults and implementation details that determine whether data survives a bad day. 2 5 Do not store Terraform state in Git or unsecured object storage; HashiCorp warns that state can expose secrets and should use locking plus secure access control [14][15]. 2 5 Do not assume VolumeSnapshots are durable away from the original PV storage system; Velero CSI docs tell operators to check provider durability behavior [10]. 2 5 Do not enable Velero backup owner references when generated backups must remain useful after the schedule is disabled or removed [8]. 2 5 Do not rely on FSB for high write databases without quiescing or app native backup verification because it is a live filesystem backup and less consistent than snapshots [11]. 2 5 Do not confuse Kubernetes API data encryption with container filesystem encryption; mounted volume data needs storage level or application level encryption [16]. 2 5 Maintenance Notes Maintenance guidance should be explicit about cadence and triggers. At minimum, recommend a routine backup health review, a scheduled restore drill, and pre change backups before Kubernetes upgrades, etcd maintenance, CSI driver changes, Velero upgrades, Argo CD upgrades, Terraform backend migration, or storage class replacement [3][4][8][12][13][15]. The exact cadence should be tied to RPO/RTO and workload criticality rather than a universal calendar, but the page should include examples such as daily scheduled namespace backups for standard workloads, more frequent backups for low RPO systems, and quarterly full restore rehearsals for critical services. 3 4 The page should also include documentation maintenance: keep the storage matrix current, update command templates when tool versions change, refresh source URLs when docs move, and preserve AI provenance metadata in frontmatter as requested by the issue. Whenever Velero, Kubernetes, Terraform, Argo CD, or a CSI driver is upgraded, rerun a small restore path before declaring the upgrade operationally complete [9][10][12][13][14][15]. 3 4 Monthly: review latest successful etcd snapshot status, Velero backup status, backup repository access, and snapshot age against RPO. 3 4 Quarterly or after major platform changes: perform a timed restore into an isolated target and compare actual recovery time and data age with RTO/RPO [17][18]. 3 4 Before Terraform backend changes: copy state to a protected backup location, reinitialize, migrate state, and verify state operations afterward [15]. 3 4 After storage driver changes: revalidate VolumeSnapshotClass behavior, CSI driver names, reclaimPolicy, volume expansion, and restore into a test namespace [6][7][10]. 3 4 After security incidents: assume backup credentials may be exposed until proven otherwise; test offline or isolated copies and restore into a clean environment [20]. 3 4 Decision Matrix To be expanded from cited source material. Reference Architecture To be expanded from cited source material. Restore Runbook To be expanded from cited source material. Failure Scenarios To be expanded from cited source material. Operational Checklist To be expanded from cited source material. Sources 1. kubernetes.io 2. kubernetes.io 3. etcd.io 4. etcd.io 5. kubernetes.io 6. kubernetes.io 7. kubernetes.io 8. velero.io 9. velero.io 10. velero.io 11. velero.io 12. velero.io 13. argo cd.readthedocs.io 14. developer.hashicorp.com 15. developer.hashicorp.com 16. kubernetes.io 17. docs.aws.amazon.com 18. docs.aws.amazon.com 19. csrc.nist.gov 20. www.cisa.gov

title: DevOps
text: DevOps Cloud, Kubernetes, CI/CD, automation, infrastructure.

title: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads
headingPath: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads
text: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads Summary Open with the practical thesis: a Kubernetes restore plan is only credible when it covers desired state, cluster API objects, persistent volume data, application level state, secrets, and control plane state separately. Argo CD uses Git repositories as the desired state source and continuously compares live state against that target, while Velero backs up Kubernetes objects and persistent volumes and etcd snapshot restore is a separate control plane recovery path [18][10][9]. Do not let the page imply that any single tool is enough to recover a production service. 18 10 Frame the operator's job as matching recovery mechanisms to failure modes and business objectives. RTO is the maximum acceptable outage duration and RPO is the maximum acceptable age of recoverable data, so backup schedules, storage location durability, restore automation, and drill cadence should be chosen to meet those targets rather than copied from a generic best practice checklist [21]. NIST CSF 2.0 treats recovery as restoration of affected assets and operations, which supports writing this page as an operational readiness guide, not merely a backup installation note [22]. 18 10 State the default architecture: GitOps for desired state, Velero for Kubernetes objects and volumes, application native procedures for databases, and etcd snapshots for the control plane [18][10][9]. 18 10 Make the reader choose explicit RPO/RTO examples such as "configuration restore within 30 minutes" or "database data loss below 15 minutes" before selecting backup frequency or tooling [21]. 18 10 Treat every restore as a production change with a rollback decision, target namespace or cluster, and validation criteria before touching live traffic [10][16]. 18 10 Decision Matrix Build the matrix around recovery surfaces. For bad manifests or accidental object deletion, start with GitOps reconciliation and Velero object restore; for persistent volume rollback, prefer CSI snapshots when the storage driver supports Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot APIs; for long term or cross provider durability, add CSI Snapshot Data Movement; for unsupported snapshot backends, consider Velero File System Backup with its beta, live filesystem, and privilege caveats; for database consistency, use application native dump, WAL, or quiesce flows alongside Velero hooks [8][11][12][13][15]. 8 11 Make the tradeoffs explicit instead of presenting a single recommendation. Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot is a point in time request that depends on CSI support, while File System Backup can cover many volume types but reads live file systems and is less point in time consistent than snapshot approaches [1][13]. For zone constrained storage, StorageClass volumeBindingMode matters because Immediate binding can provision before Pod scheduling constraints are known, whereas WaitForFirstConsumer delays binding until the workload exists [3]. 8 11 Use GitOps and kubectl apply/diff when the desired state in Git is correct and the failure is drift or accidental Kubernetes object deletion [8][18]. 8 11 Use Velero CSI snapshot backup for PVC backed data when the CSI driver and VolumeSnapshotClass are known to work in the target cluster [1][11]. 8 11 Use CSI Snapshot Data Movement when storage snapshots are not durable enough, must survive storage system failure, or need to move across providers [12]. 8 11 Use File System Backup only with a documented acceptance of beta status, live filesystem consistency, hostPath limitations, and node agent security implications [13]. 8 11 Use etcd snapshot restore only for control plane disaster recovery, and treat it as creation of a new logical cluster, not a live object level rollback [9]. 8 11 Reference Architecture Describe the architecture as six layers. Layer 1 is Git, where Kubernetes manifests, Helm values, Kustomize overlays, and Argo CD application definitions live. Layer 2 is Kubernetes API object backup, where Velero captures API resources into object storage. Layer 3 is PV data protection, using CSI snapshots, CSI snapshot data movement, or File System Backup depending on storage support. Layer 4 is application consistency, where backup hooks or database native dumps flush or freeze state before a snapshot. Layer 5 is secrets and encryption, where API resource encryption and RBAC are prerequisites for safely preserving sensitive objects. Layer 6 is control plane etcd snapshots for cluster state disasters [18][10][11][12][15][6][7][9]. 18 10 Show the data flow in prose: scheduled Velero backups create timestamped recovery points, write Kubernetes object archives to object storage, and snapshot or copy volume data when configured [10][17]. During restore, the backup storage location should be treated as the source of truth and can be set read only to avoid accidental creation or deletion while recovery is underway [10]. The target restore should begin in an isolated namespace or clean cluster unless the incident requires an explicitly approved in place update [16]. 18 10 Keep Git repositories independently protected; GitOps cannot restore a cluster if the desired state repository, secrets workflow, or deployment keys are unavailable [18]. 18 10 Keep object storage for Velero backups separate from the failed cluster and restrict mutation during restore windows [10]. 18 10 Document each StorageClass reclaimPolicy and volumeBindingMode, because deletion defaults and topology binding affect whether a restored workload can attach storage [2][3]. 18 10 Include an etcd snapshot path only for control plane rebuild scenarios, with revision bumping guidance for Kubernetes consumers [9]. 18 10 Restore Runbook Start the runbook with triage, not commands. Identify the failure type, decide whether the target is a new cluster, a new namespace, or an in place repair, freeze unrelated deploys, and preserve evidence such as backup names, namespace names, PVC names, StorageClass details, and the desired RPO/RTO. Velero restores are non destructive by default and skip existing resources unless an update policy is selected, so the safest default drill is a clean namespace or clean cluster restore with namespace mapping [16][10]. 16 10 Then provide command templates with warnings. Supported examples include velero restore create <RESTORE NAME from backup <BACKUP NAME namespace mappings old ns:new ns , velero restore create <RESTORE NAME from backup <BACKUP NAME parallel files download <NUM wait for fs restore or CSI snapshot data movement cases, and kubectl diff f <directory followed by kubectl apply f <directory for declarative object reconstruction [16][8]. For etcd, show only a fenced, clearly labeled control plane recovery template such as etcdctl snapshot save snapshot.db and etcdutl snapshot restore snapshot.db bump revision <N mark compacted data dir <output dir ; note that this starts a new logical cluster and must not be treated as a live namespace rollback [9]. 16 10 Preflight: record backup name, target cluster, target namespace, affected PVCs, StorageClass, VolumeSnapshotClass, application version, and traffic cutover condition [16]. 16 10 Restore safely first: use namespace mapping into a non serving namespace where possible, then validate pods, PVC binding, service discovery, and application integrity before exposing traffic [16]. 16 10 For Argo CD state, use the documented argocd admin export and argocd admin import path, and include the namespace flag when Argo CD is not installed in the default namespace [19]. 16 10 For declarative manifests, use kubectl diff before kubectl apply , and avoid mixing apply with create or replace management methods [8]. 16 10 For etcd restore, require a separate control plane change ticket and operator pair review because snapshot restore creates a new logical cluster and changes member identities [9]. 16 10 Failure Scenarios Cover common failure scenarios with the first safe move, the restore method, and the validation test. For accidental manifest deletion or drift, use GitOps reconciliation or declarative apply, then check that live state matches the desired commit [18][8]. For namespace level deletion, use Velero resource filters or namespace mapping to restore into a controlled target and validate CRDs, RBAC, Services, and PVC binding before exposing clients [14][16]. For a corrupted PVC, choose CSI snapshot restore or data movement if supported; otherwise consider FSB or application native restore with the documented consistency limitations [1][11][12][13]. 18 8 Include less obvious failures that create false confidence. If a database accepted writes during a volume snapshot and no application hook or logical backup was used, the storage image may not meet the application's consistency expectations even if the volume attaches successfully [15][10]. If a cluster restore targets a newer or different Kubernetes API surface, Velero requires the backed up API group/version to exist in the target cluster, so restore tests must include CRDs and operators, not only Deployments and Services [10]. If the incident is control plane loss, etcd disaster recovery is a separate path that uses a snapshot database to form a new logical cluster [9]. 18 8 Bad deploy or config drift: revert Git commit or apply known good manifests, then allow Argo CD to sync or use kubectl apply with diff preview [18][8]. 18 8 Deleted namespace: restore with namespace mappings into a quarantine namespace first, then validate application and data before traffic cutover [16]. 18 8 PV corruption: use CSI snapshot or data mover when available; use FSB only after accepting beta status and live filesystem limitations [11][12][13]. 18 8 Secret exposure: rotate credentials after restore and verify encryption at rest and RBAC before treating restored Secrets as safe [6][7]. 18 8 Control plane failure: follow etcd snapshot restore, revision bump, and compaction guidance; do not attempt to solve it with namespace level Velero restore alone [9]. 18 8 Operational Checklist Write this as a checklist that an on call or platform owner can run monthly and before risky migrations. Confirm that every production namespace has a declared recovery owner, RPO, RTO, backup schedule, retention policy, restore target, and validation test. Check that Velero scheduled backups are producing timestamped recovery points, TTLs match retention policy, and filters are scoped to the intended namespaces and resource kinds [17][14][10]. 17 14 Add storage and workload checks that prevent surprises. Inventory StorageClasses for reclaimPolicy, volumeBindingMode, expansion settings, and snapshot support; ensure StatefulSet volume retention is understood; verify that no persistent application data depends on Docker writable layers or Kubernetes ephemeral assumptions; and confirm that backup storage access is separated from normal cluster credentials [3][4][20][13]. Kubernetes CronJobs can run operational jobs, but missed schedule behavior and controller cadence mean successful backup completion needs monitoring rather than blind trust in cron syntax [5]. 17 14 Daily or per shift: check latest backup completion, age, size trend, and error count; alert when newest backup age exceeds the declared RPO [21]. 17 14 Weekly: perform at least one namespace mapped restore of a representative workload and record elapsed restore time against RTO [16][21]. 17 14 Monthly: restore a PVC backed StatefulSet and verify application level data, not only Pod readiness [4][11][13]. 17 14 Quarterly: run a control plane tabletop covering etcd snapshot location, restore commands, revision bump decision, and certificate or kubeconfig dependencies [9]. 17 14 After every storage driver or CSI driver change: re test VolumeSnapshotClass behavior and Velero restore into a non serving namespace [1][11][3]. 17 14 Common Pitfalls Lead with false assumptions. A StatefulSet keeps sticky Pod identity and stable storage, and Kubernetes intentionally avoids deleting associated volumes when scaling down or deleting the StatefulSet, but that is a data safety behavior, not a backup strategy [4]. Docker's writable container layer is also not a persistence layer: Docker documents that it does not persist after container deletion and is a poor choice for write intensive data, so the page should tell readers to use volumes or Kubernetes PVs for data that matters [20]. 4 20 Then cover restore specific traps. Velero backups are not strictly atomic, so highly active object changes can fall outside a backup [10]. FSB can back up many volume types but is beta quality, less consistent than snapshot approaches, and may require privileged node agent behavior in some environments [13]. Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd by default, which means backup archives containing Secrets require encryption, least privilege access, and rotation procedures after exposure [6][7]. 4 20 Do not assume "pod is Ready" means "data restored correctly"; require application specific integrity checks [10][15]. 4 20 Do not enable broad restore filters in production without a target namespace or clean cluster plan [14][16]. 4 20 Do not keep only storage system snapshots when the storage system itself is in the failure domain; use data movement or object storage where durability requirements demand it [12]. 4 20 Do not rely on CronJob alone for backup assurance; monitor missed runs and backup freshness against RPO [5][21]. 4 20 Do not restore etcd snapshots casually; official guidance treats restore as a new logical cluster and recommends revision bumping for Kubernetes contexts [9]. 4 20 Maintenance Notes Make maintenance concrete. Update the page whenever Kubernetes, Velero, Argo CD, CSI drivers, StorageClasses, VolumeSnapshotClasses, backup bucket policy, encryption configuration, or database engine versions change. Velero restore depends on backed up API group/version availability in the target cluster, so CRD and operator upgrade plans must include restore validation, not just forward migration testing [10]. StorageClass changes need backup review because reclaimPolicy, volumeBindingMode, expansion behavior, and mount options directly affect restore outcomes [3]. 10 3 Define a practical cadence: daily backup freshness checks, weekly representative restores, monthly stateful workload restores, quarterly etcd disaster recovery tabletop, and pre migration restore drills before Kubernetes or storage upgrades. Use the NIST and DR planning framing to keep the focus on readiness: recovery actions should be ready before an incident, and RTO/RPO results from drills should feed improvements to schedules, automation, and documentation [22][21]. 10 3 Maintain a compatibility table for Kubernetes version, Velero version, CSI driver, VolumeSnapshotClass, StorageClass, object storage, and encryption settings [10][11][1][3]. 10 3 Record the last successful restore date and elapsed restore time for each critical workload, not merely the last successful backup time [21]. 10 3 Review Velero backup TTL and schedule owner reference choices so GitOps tooling does not prune or fight day two Backup and Restore resources unexpectedly [17]. 10 3 Review Secrets handling after every key management or RBAC change, and rotate restored credentials after suspected exposure [6][7]. 10 3 Keep Argo CD export/import commands pinned to the running version and namespace used in the cluster [19]. 10 3 Sources 1. kubernetes.io 2. kubernetes.io 3. kubernetes.io 4. kubernetes.io 5. kubernetes.io 6. kubernetes.io 7. kubernetes.io 8. kubernetes.io 9. etcd.io 10. velero.io 11. velero.io 12. velero.io 13. velero.io 14. velero.io 15. velero.io 16. velero.io 17. velero.io 18. argo cd.readthedocs.io 19. argo cd.readthedocs.io 20. docs.docker.com 21. docs.cloud.google.com 22. nvlpubs.nist.gov
